problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

phase.name: Phase 1: First Solid Mediums
phase.description: Shorter statements, one main invariant, and quick pattern payoff.

problems.whySolve: This is one of the cleanest introductions to a moving constraint, and the same rhythm shows up across many string mediums.
problems.whatYouPractice: Maintaining window state, knowing when to shrink, and separating the invariant from the bookkeeping.
problems.overview: Keep a live substring valid while repeated characters force you to adjust its left edge.
problems.interestNote: A classic first Medium because the statement is short but the technique scales far beyond this problem.
problems.caveat: The idea is clean, but it is easy to get the left boundary updates slightly wrong.
problems.statementStyle: Short string prompt with one dominant condition.
problems.expectedMentalModel: Protect one running invariant instead of restarting work from scratch.

problems.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement

phase.name: Phase 1: First Solid Mediums
phase.description: Shorter statements, one main invariant, and quick pattern payoff.

problems.whySolve: It deepens the sliding-window habit without turning into a long statement or a niche trick.
problems.whatYouPractice: Reasoning about window validity, frequency counts, and when an approximate summary is enough to move forward.
problems.overview: Grow and trim a substring while asking how much editing pressure the current window can absorb.

problems.caveat: Many first attempts over-correct the frequency logic and make the window harder than it needs to be.
problems.statementStyle: Compact string prompt with a single tolerance rule.
problems.expectedMentalModel: A valid window can survive some pressure; you do not need to rebuild the whole story each step.

problems.interestNote: A very practical next-step window Medium for interview preparation.
problems.caveat: The mistake pattern is predictable: people track too much instead of only the counts that can break validity.
problems.statementStyle: Short array prompt with one local frequency rule.
problems.expectedMentalModel: Only shrink when the current window violates the cap, and only as much as needed to restore legality.

problems.title: Count Subarrays Where Max Element Appears at Least K Times

phase.name: Phase 1: First Solid Mediums
phase.description: Shorter statements, one main invariant, and quick pattern payoff.

problems.whySolve: It is a worthwhile counting Medium because it nudges you away from brute-force window enumeration toward more structural counting.
problems.whatYouPractice: Counting without explicit enumeration, turning a condition into positional reasoning, and using monotonic movement to compress work.
problems.overview: Count valid subarrays by reasoning about where the important maximum-value occurrences sit instead of enumerating every range.
problems.interestNote: Good for strengthening the 'count, do not list' habit that many later array problems rely on.
problems.caveat: The challenge is not the statement; it is resisting the urge to inspect every candidate subarray directly.
problems.statementStyle: Readable array-counting prompt with one highlighted element.
problems.expectedMentalModel: Once the key occurrence positions are understood, whole groups of subarrays can be counted at once.

problems.title: Maximum Points in an Archery Competition

phase.name: Phase 5: State Space Search
phase.description: Backtracking, DAG exploration, and choice-making under constraints.

problems.whySolve: It is one of the more approachable backtracking Mediums because the choices are concrete and the search space has a visible story.
problems.whatYouPractice: State branching, budget tracking, and deciding when a search problem is small enough to explore directly.
problems.overview: Distribute a limited resource across scoring targets and search for the best legal allocation.
problems.interestNote: A useful 'backtracking can still feel friendly' problem.
problems.caveat: The branching is manageable, but the search only stays readable if the state representation is disciplined from the start.
problems.statementStyle: Concrete resource-allocation prompt with a bounded search space.
problems.expectedMentalModel: Each target is a choice point: spend enough to win it or skip it, and keep the remaining budget honest.

problems.title: Count Ways To Build Good Strings

phase.name: Phase 6: Intro Dynamic Programming
phase.description: Decision chains and manageable state transitions that start to feel like real DP.

problems.whySolve: It is an approachable DP counting problem because the state is simple and the recurrence follows directly from the allowed moves.
problems.whatYouPractice: 1D counting DP, state transitions on length, and distinguishing 'how many ways' from 'is it possible.'
problems.overview: Count how many valid strings can be built when each move increases length by one of two allowed step sizes.
problems.interestNote: A very good newer DP ramp problem if classic coin-style prompts feel too abstract.
problems.caveat: The recurrence is friendly, but only once each length is treated as a state with incoming contributions from smaller lengths.
problems.statementStyle: Short constructive-counting prompt with explicit move sizes.
problems.expectedMentalModel: Length is the state; every valid previous length contributes forward if one of the allowed jumps can reach the current one.
